title: Selling Equipment — Gain or Loss
event: Reyes Co. sells equipment that cost $42,000 and has accumulated depreciation of $30,000 (book value $12,000). Prepare the entry.

lines.label: proceeds
lines.trap: Compare proceeds to BOOK VALUE, not to original cost — cost is history.

lines.label: wiped clean
lines.why: The contra-asset dies with the asset — debit it away.
lines.trap: Forgetting this line is the #1 disposal error; the entry won't balance without it.

lines.label: original cost off
lines.why: The asset leaves at its ORIGINAL cost.
lines.trap: Credit full cost — never the 12,000 book value; BV is a computed number, not an account.

lines.label: 15,000 − 12,000
lines.trap: The gain is vs book value. Comparing to the 42,000 cost would show a phantom 27,000 loss.

lines.label: proceeds
lines.trap: Below book value — the debit side needs a loss to balance.

lines.label: wiped clean
lines.trap: Same line, gain or loss — accumulated depreciation always clears at disposal.

lines.label: 12,000 − 9,500
lines.trap: The loss plugs the debit side — if your plug lands on the credit side, you've got a gain.

lines.label: original cost off
lines.trap: Cost and accumulated depreciation BOTH leave — book value exits in two pieces.

memorize.body: Gain/(loss) = proceeds − book value. Book value = cost − accumulated depreciation.
If the sale happens mid-year, update depreciation TO THE SALE DATE first — then compute book value.

questions.prompt: Cost $42,000, accumulated depreciation $30,000, sold for $15,000. Gain = ?

distractors.feedback: You compared proceeds to original COST. Gains measure against BOOK VALUE: 15,000 − 12,000 = 3,000.
That's the cash received. The gain is only the excess over the 12,000 book value.

build.scaffold: Four lines every time: cash in, accumulated depreciation out, asset out at cost, gain/loss plugs.
